prior_decisions.phase_6_refusal_q12_q13: Refusals indexed in .protostar/refusals.jsonl (append-only, sourceOfTruth discriminator). Phase 8 added EvaluationResult variant.
prior_decisions.phase_6_cancel_wiring: apps/factory-cli/src/cancel.ts already installs SIGINT + CANCEL-sentinel cooperative cancel for an in-flight run. OP-04 needs the OUT-OF-PROCESS surface.
prior_decisions.phase_4_journal: packages/execution exposes TaskJournalEvent stream + reduceJournalToSnapshot. Snapshot.json is the deterministic projection of the journal. OP-03 resume must rebuild from these.
prior_decisions.phase_5_authority_brand: DeliveryAuthorization is a Phase 5 brand minted only when both mechanical and model review pass. OP-06 deliver must consume an existing authorization; cannot mint one.
prior_decisions.phase_7_delivery_runtime: packages/delivery-runtime owns Octokit + push (network-only). apps/factory-cli persists ci-events.jsonl. OP-06 reuses that runtime entrypoint.
prior_decisions.phase_8_evolution_chain: .protostar/evolution/{lineageId}.jsonl is append-only and MUST survive run pruning (Phase 8 Q-14 lock).
prior_decisions.constraint_authority: Only apps/factory-cli and packages/repo do filesystem I/O. Subcommand modules live in apps/factory-cli/src/.
prior_decisions.constraint_runtime_deps: Minimal external runtime deps (PROJECT.md). Adding a CLI argument-parser library requires an explicit lock note. Current parseArgs is hand-rolled.
prior_decisions.constraint_archetype: Only cosmetic-tweak archetype is wired for v0.1.
code_landmarks: apps/factory-cli/src/main.ts:2517 — parseArgs (single 'run' command today; needs subcommand router)
packages/execution/src/snapshot.ts — reduceJournalToSnapshot + serializeSnapshot (deterministic JSON via sortJsonValue)
packages/execution/src/orphan-replay.ts — replayOrphanedTasks (Phase 4 — already exists for resume)
packages/artifacts/src/index.ts — FactoryRunManifest { runId, intentId, status, createdAt, stages }; status enum lacks 'cancelled' today

.protostar/runs/<runId>/{manifest.json, intent.json, plan.json, execution-plan.json, planning-mission.txt, review-mission.txt, review-gate.json, piles/<kind>/iter-<N>/, execution/{journal.jsonl,snapshot.json}, evolution/snapshot.json, ci-events.jsonl} — observed shape
.protostar/refusals.jsonl + .protostar/evolution/{lineageId}.jsonl — workspace-level append-only indexes that must survive prune

questions.title: Subcommand router refactor
questions.context: Today parseArgs (apps/factory-cli/src/main.ts:2517) accepts only `run`. OP-01..OP-08 introduce 6+ new subcommands (status, resume, cancel, inspect, deliver, prune). Where does the dispatch live?

options.label: Extend parseArgs in-place
options.description: Add cases to the existing switch in main.ts. Lowest churn; main.ts continues to grow (~3000 LoC already) and command handlers stay co-located with run-loop code.

options.label: Per-command modules under apps/factory-cli/src/commands/
options.description: New folder commands/{run,status,resume,cancel,inspect,deliver,prune}.ts. Each exports `parse(rest)` and `execute(opts)`. main.ts becomes a thin dispatcher. Slightly more files, much smaller blast radius per command, easier to test in isolation.

options.label: Single commands.ts with a registry table
options.description: One new file that exports `{ name, parse, execute }` records; main.ts iterates the table. Middle ground.

questions.title: Argument parser: hand-rolled vs library
questions.context: Current parseArgs is hand-rolled and works fine for one command. Six more subcommands each with their own flags is more surface. PROJECT.md Constraints say minimal runtime deps; adding a parser library needs an explicit lock note.

options.label: Stay hand-rolled, share a parseFlags helper
options.description: Extract the flag-parser already in main.ts into a shared util; per-command parse functions call it then validate. No new dep. More boilerplate; auditable.

options.label: Add `commander` (or `@commander-js/extra-typings`)
options.description: Mature, types-friendly, ~80KB. Subcommand DSL eliminates router boilerplate. New runtime dep — must be added to PROJECT.md Constraints lock revision.

options.label: Add a tiny purpose-built parser (e.g., `mri` or `arg`)
options.description: <10KB; no subcommand DSL but clean flag parsing. Same lock note required.

questions.title: Exit-code taxonomy
questions.context: OP-07 says output must be pipeable. Pipeable in shell = predictable exit codes. Today the `run` command uses 0 for ok and 1 for any error.

options.label: Binary 0/1
options.description: Keep current behavior across all subcommands. Simplest; consumer scripts can't distinguish 'not found' from 'arg error' from 'cancelled'.

options.label: Curated taxonomy
options.description: 0 success, 1 generic error, 2 usage/arg error, 3 not-found, 4 conflict, 5 cancelled-by-operator, 6 not-resumable. Exit codes are part of OP-07's stable contract; document in --help.

options.label: Exit code via JSON only
options.description: Always exit 0; signal outcome via `{ ok: false, code }` in stdout JSON. Hostile to shell pipelines (`set -e`). Probably wrong for operator surface.

questions.title: stdout vs stderr discipline
questions.context: OP-07 says output is JSON-stable so it's pipeable. That implies a clean `cmd ... | jq` story. Anything chatty must not pollute stdout.

options.label: Strict: stdout = data only; stderr = everything else
options.description: Progress, banners, warnings → stderr. stdout always parses as a single JSON value. Diagnostic logging behind --verbose to stderr. Cleanest pipe story.

options.label: Strict for new subcommands; leave `run` as-is
options.description: `run` already logs progress to stdout. Touching it risks regressing v0.1. New subcommands adopt strict discipline; `run` cleaned up later.

options.label: Loose: stdout for both, JSON one-line-per-event
options.description: ndjson on stdout. Friendlier for streaming `status --watch` later but kills the simple `cmd | jq .` story.

questions.title: Default output mode for status
questions.context: OP-02 lists last N runs with verdict + archetype + duration; --run <id> prints manifest summary. OP-07 says JSON-stable. What does a bare `protostar-factory status` print?

options.label: JSON by default
options.description: `status` always emits a JSON object. Human readability is a `--human` flag. Maximally pipeable; hostile to first-time CLI users.

options.label: Human by default, --json flag
options.description: Bare `status` prints a fixed-width text table; `--json` emits the stable JSON shape. TTY-friendly default; opt-in for scripting.

options.label: TTY-aware auto-switch
options.description: If stdout is a TTY → human; else → JSON. Magical (same command, two shapes — surprises CI).

questions.title: Default N for `status` (no --run)
questions.context: OP-02: 'last N runs'. Today `.protostar/runs/` for this project already has dozens of run dirs. We need a default cap.

options.description: Fits a typical terminal screen. Use `--limit N` (default 10) and `--all` to disable cap.
Bigger window for active dogfood loops (Phase 10 wants ≥10 consecutive runs).

options.label: Cap by duration, not count
options.description: Default `--since 24h`; `--limit` overrides. Better matches operator mental model. More code; needs duration parser shared with prune.

questions.title: Status row schema
questions.context: What columns/fields make up one row of `status`? Each must be derivable from the manifest + on-disk artifacts; the schema is a public OP-07 contract.

options.label: Minimal — just OP-02's literal asks
options.description: { runId, archetype, verdict, durationMs }. Smallest surface to lock. Operator must inspect for anything else.

options.label: Full operator dashboard
options.description: { runId, archetype, status, reviewVerdict, evaluationVerdict, lineageId, generation, prUrl?, durationMs, createdAt }. Everything an operator usually wants in one shot. Bigger schema-stability commitment.

options.label: Tiered — minimal default, --full opt-in
options.description: Bare `status` returns (a). `status --full` returns (b). Two schemas to lock but each smaller.

questions.title: Run discovery: scan vs index
questions.context: How does `status` enumerate runs? Today there is no top-level index; runs live under `.protostar/runs/<runId>/`. Scanning works at v0.1 scale; degrades at scale.

options.label: Directory scan, sorted by mtime
options.description: `readdir` + `stat`; sort by mtime desc; read each manifest.json lazily for the requested page. Trivial; O(n) per status call.

options.label: Append-only `.protostar/runs.jsonl` index
options.description: Symmetric with refusals.jsonl + evolution/{lineage}.jsonl. One line per run-create. Adds a write-side surface to the run loop.

options.label: Scan now, indexed in Phase 10 if needed
options.description: Ship (a) for v0.1; revisit if dogfood (≥10 runs) shows latency. Document as a scaling concern.

questions.title: Mid-run / corrupted manifest handling
questions.context: A killed run leaves manifest.json non-terminal ('running'). A crashed-mid-write run may have partial JSON. status must not crash on either.

options.label: Best-effort row with `state: 'unknown'`
options.description: Wrap each manifest read in try/catch; on parse failure emit `{ runId, state: 'unknown', error }` and continue. Soft-fail; operator notices.

options.label: Compute liveness from journal + sentinel
options.description: manifest 'running' AND no recent journal append AND no CANCEL → mark `state: 'orphaned'`. Communicates more truth. Resume command needs the same logic — share a helper.

options.label: Hard-fail on first corrupt row
options.description: Surface the bug. Punishes the operator for v0.1 crashes. Probably wrong.

questions.title: Default inspect view
questions.context: OP-05: 'pretty-print the run bundle (manifest, stage records, artifacts)'. A run bundle includes manifest.json, plan.json, review-gate.json, evaluation-report.json, evolution/snapshot.json, ci-events.jsonl tail, refusals — that's a lot of JSON.

options.label: Composite JSON: full bundle inlined
options.description: `inspect <id>` emits a single JSON object with every artifact inlined under stable keys ({ manifest, planning, execution, review, evaluation, delivery, refusals }). Largest payload; one-shot for jq.

options.label: Manifest + index of artifact paths
options.description: `inspect <id>` emits manifest + `{ artifacts: [{ stage, kind, path, sha256? }] }`. Operator follows up with `cat`/`jq` on individual files. Smaller surface.

options.label: Composite, but stage-sliceable
options.description: Default = (a). `inspect <id> --stage execution` returns just the execution slice. Same keys/sub-shape regardless of slice.

questions.title: Trace inclusion (pile traces are big)
questions.context: Phase 6 Q-08 locked always-on trace.json per pile invocation. These can be 10s of MB across iterations. Including by default in inspect blows up the JSON.

options.label: Exclude traces by default; include via --full or --include-traces
options.description: Default inspect references traces by path under `artifacts[]`; flag inlines them. Keeps default output bounded; opt-in for deep audits.

options.label: Always reference, never inline
options.description: Traces NEVER inlined; only referenced by path + sha256. `cat <path> | jq` is the way to read them. Smallest, most predictable.

options.label: Always inline
options.description: Operator gets one JSON. Pathological at scale.

questions.title: Field ordering / canonicalization
questions.context: OP-07 'JSON-stable so pipeable'. Two things stability could mean: schema (keys exist) vs serialization (byte-identical for identical inputs).

options.label: Sorted keys (sortJsonValue pattern from snapshot.ts)
options.description: Reuse packages/execution/src/snapshot.ts's sortJsonValue to alphabetize keys recursively. Byte-stable across machines. Same approach Phase 4 already uses for snapshot.json.

options.label: Schema-fixed insertion order
options.description: Hand-author serialization to a documented field order. Reads more naturally to humans but harder to keep stable across refactors.

options.label: JSON.stringify default (insertion order)
options.description: No canonicalization. Stability depends on construction order. Easy to regress.

questions.title: What stages can resume from?
questions.context: Phase 4 task journal supports re-deriving execution state via reduceJournalToSnapshot + replayOrphanedTasks. Other stages (planning pile, review pile, evaluation pile) write iter-N folders but have no journal — they're effectively idempotent re-runs.

options.label: Execution-only resume
options.description: `resume <id>` ONLY for runs killed mid-execution. Other states refuse with exit code 6. Scope-minimal; matches OP-03 literal text ('from its task journal').

options.label: Stage-aware resume
options.description: Resume jumps to the latest non-terminal stage. Mid-execution → replay journal. Mid-review → re-invoke review pile from latest iter. Mid-evaluation → re-invoke evaluation pile. More logic; more failure modes; closer to operator expectation.

options.label: (a) for v0.1 + clear refusal message pointing at re-run
options.description: Execution-only; non-execution states exit 6 with a message: 'this run can be re-driven by `protostar-factory run --intent-draft <path> --run-id <id>` (idempotent on completed stages)'. Phase 10 may upgrade to (b).

questions.title: Re-execution semantics for a partial journal
questions.context: Journal has events for tasks T1,T2,T3 with T2 stuck in 'running' (no terminal event). What does resume do?

options.label: Replay only orphaned tasks (replayOrphanedTasks)
options.description: Phase 4 already exposes replayOrphanedTasks. Re-execute T2 (and any later tasks) from its last seq. Preserves successful T1/T3.

options.label: Re-run the whole plan
options.description: Discard journal; start fresh task graph. Simpler but loses successful work. Probably wrong given Phase 4's existing replay helper.

options.label: Operator chooses: --from <taskId> or default to (a)
options.description: Default = (a); escape hatch lets operator force re-run from a specific task.

questions.title: Cancel sentinel on resume
questions.context: If runs/{id}/CANCEL exists, the run was either explicitly cancelled or sentinel-tripped. installCancelWiring already exposes unlinkSentinelOnResume.

options.label: Auto-unlink on resume
options.description: Use unlinkSentinelOnResume helper. Cancel was a transient signal; resume implies operator wants forward motion. Simplest.

options.label: Refuse if sentinel present unless --force
options.description: Cancel may have been deliberate; require explicit override. Safer. Adds an exit code (4: conflict).

options.label: Refuse if manifest.status === 'cancelled'; auto-unlink otherwise
options.description: Distinguish operator-cancel (terminal — refuse) from in-flight-sentinel-trip (transient — auto-unlink). Requires Q-18's manifest-status decision.

questions.title: Out-of-process cancel mechanism
questions.context: installCancelWiring (apps/factory-cli/src/cancel.ts) handles in-process SIGINT and reads the CANCEL sentinel between tasks. OP-04's `protostar-factory cancel <runId>` runs in a SEPARATE process and must signal the running one.

options.label: Sentinel-only
options.description: `cancel <runId>` writes runs/<id>/CANCEL and exits. The in-flight process picks it up between tasks. Simplest; latency = current-task duration. Existing cancel wiring already supports this.

options.label: PID file + SIGTERM, fall back to sentinel
options.description: run loop writes runs/<id>/run.pid at start; cancel sends SIGTERM to PID, then writes sentinel as belt-and-suspenders. Lower latency. Adds PID lifecycle complexity (stale PID, cross-host invalid).

options.label: Sentinel + manifest mark
options.description: Write CANCEL sentinel AND mark manifest status='cancelling'. status command shows 'cancelling' until run loop confirms 'cancelled'. Better operator visibility.

questions.title: Cancel against an already-finished run
questions.context: `cancel <runId>` against a run whose manifest.status is 'completed' or 'blocked' — what happens?

options.label: No-op success (exit 0)
options.description: Cancel is idempotent; already-terminal = nothing to do. Operator-friendly.

options.label: Refuse with exit code 4 (conflict)
options.description: Surface the truth: there was nothing to cancel. Forces operator to re-read state. Stricter.

options.label: Refuse for completed; no-op for blocked
options.description: Differentiate by terminality. Probably more conceptual overhead than it's worth.

questions.title: FactoryRunStatus enum extension
questions.context: packages/artifacts/src/index.ts FactoryRunStatus is currently 'created' | 'running' | 'blocked' | 'repairing' | 'ready-to-release' | 'completed'. Phase 9 introduces cancel + resume + delivery-gated states.

options.label: Add 'cancelled' only
options.description: Minimum to land OP-04. Resume keeps 'running'; deliver-gating reuses 'ready-to-release'. Cleanest schema bump.

options.label: Add 'cancelled' + 'cancelling'
options.description: Two-step cancel (sentinel set → loop confirms) gives status visibility into in-flight cancels. Pairs with Q-16(c).

options.label: Add 'cancelled' + 'orphaned'
options.description: 'orphaned' for runs whose journal stalled and process is gone (Q-09 needs this notion). status surfaces it; resume picks it up.

options.label: Add 'cancelled' + 'cancelling' + 'orphaned'
options.description: Full set. Largest schema bump; clearest operator surface. Schema-stability cost is real (admission-e2e contract test must lock).

questions.title: runId validation on operator surfaces
questions.context: All new commands take a <runId>. Today runIds look like `run_20260425200234_dark_factory_scaffold`. Phase 9 must reject path-traversal and shell-injection attempts.

options.label: Strict regex `^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,128}$`
options.description: Same shape as the existing branch-name validator. Reject anything else with exit 2. Validate at the parse layer; planner/inspect/etc. take a branded `RunId` type.

options.label: Path-relative check only
options.description: Resolve runs/<id> and ensure it stays inside .protostar/runs/. Catches traversal but allows weird characters that may break shell pipes.

options.label: Both: regex + path-confinement
options.description: Belt-and-suspenders; cheap. Matches Phase 5 mint-brand-validator pattern.

questions.title: When does `deliver <runId>` apply?
questions.context: Phase 7 delivery runs INSIDE the loop when DeliveryAuthorization is minted. OP-06 'explicit delivery trigger when delivery is gated to operator action' implies a config-gated mode where the loop stops at ready-to-release without auto-delivering.

options.label: Config flag: factory-config.json `delivery.mode: 'auto' | 'gated'`
options.description: Default 'auto' (existing Phase 7 behavior). 'gated' makes the loop pause at 'ready-to-release'; operator runs `deliver <runId>` to push. CLI override `--delivery-mode <auto|gated>` per Phase 6 Q-04.

options.label: deliver is idempotent and works regardless of mode
options.description: deliver works even after auto-delivery succeeded — no-op success. Useful for retry-after-failure too. v0.1 stays auto-only and OP-06 just exposes a retry surface.

options.label: Both: gated mode + retry idempotency
options.description: Gated mode + `deliver` works for retry too. Largest scope; cleanest operator story.

questions.title: Authority loading for `deliver`
questions.context: Phase 5 DeliveryAuthorization is a runtime-minted brand. To re-execute delivery from a separate process, the brand must be reconstituted from disk artifacts.

options.label: Persist serialized authorization at runs/<id>/delivery/authorization.json; deliver re-mints via the validator
options.description: On mint, factory-cli writes the authorization payload. deliver reads, runs the same brand-mint validator, gets a fresh brand. No 'trust the disk' shortcut.

options.label: Refuse if loop didn't pre-mint
options.description: deliver only works for runs whose loop reached ready-to-release. No re-mint logic; just a filesystem flag check. Smaller surface; loses some retry stories.

options.label: Re-derive authorization from review-gate.json on demand
options.description: deliver re-runs Phase 5's validator over the persisted ReviewGate. Same as (a) but no separate authorization.json — review-gate.json is the source of truth.

questions.title: Prune surface and safety
questions.context: .protostar/refusals.jsonl + .protostar/evolution/{lineageId}.jsonl MUST survive prune (Phase 8 Q-14 lock). Active runs must not be deleted. OP-08 phrasing: 'documented prune recipe (or `prune --older-than <duration>`)'.

options.label: Documented `find` recipe in README; no command
options.description: README ships a copy-pasteable `find .protostar/runs -mtime +N -type d ... -exec rm -rf` snippet. Zero new code; relies on operator discipline. OP-08 'or' reading.

options.label: `protostar-factory prune --older-than <duration> [--dry-run] [--archetype X]`
options.description: Full subcommand: parses duration (1d, 7d, 24h), enumerates run dirs, filters by mtime + active-status guard (refuses to delete non-terminal), preserves workspace-level append-only files. --dry-run by default; --confirm to actually delete. Most code; safest.

options.label: Recipe + a thin `prune --dry-run-only` lister
options.description: Subcommand only LISTS prune candidates as JSON; actual deletion is the documented `find` recipe operator pipes the JSON into. Splits responsibility: factory owns 'what is safe to prune', operator owns 'rm'.
